section.title: Greeting and Thanksgiving
section.sectionRationale: Before Paul confronts a single fault in this troubled church, he grounds the Corinthians in grace. The opening names them for what God has made them, sanctified in Christ and called to be holy, and then gives thanks for the grace, speech, knowledge, and gifts poured out among them. The paragraph closes on the note that will carry the whole letter: God is faithful, and He will keep them blameless to the end. Every correction that follows rests on this foundation of grace.
section.thesis: The divided, immature church at Corinth is addressed first as the sanctified people of God, enriched by grace and kept by the faithfulness of God, who has called them into fellowship with His Son and will sustain them blameless to the day of Christ.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the greeting to a sanctified church; thanksgiving for the grace and gifts given in Christ; and the ground of assurance, the faithful God who will keep them blameless to the end.

units.label: The greeting
units.note: an apostle to the sanctified church

verses.text: Paul, called to be an apostle of Christ Jesus by the will of God, and our brother Sosthenes,

To the church of God in Corinth, to those {{hagiazo|sanctified}} in Christ Jesus and called to be holy, together with all those everywhere who call on the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, their Lord and ours:
Grace and peace to you from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

units.label: Thanksgiving for grace
units.note: enriched in every way, lacking no gift

verses.text: I always thank my God for you because of the {{charis|grace}} He has given you in Christ Jesus.
For in Him you have been enriched in every way, in all speech and all knowledge,
because our testimony about Christ was confirmed in you.
Therefore you do not lack any {{charisma|spiritual gift}} as you eagerly await the revelation of our Lord Jesus Christ.
He will sustain you to the end, so that you will be blameless on the day of our Lord Jesus Christ.
God, who has called you into {{koinonia|fellowship}} with His Son Jesus Christ our Lord, is {{pistos|faithful}}.

hagiazo.gloss: To sanctify, to set apart as holy, to consecrate to God. Paul addresses the Corinthians as already sanctified in Christ Jesus, a settled status given by God, and then calls them to become in practice what they already are, holy. Their sanctification is rooted not in their own performance, which the letter will expose as badly flawed, but in their union with Christ.

charis.gloss: Grace, unearned favor, the free gift of God. It frames the whole greeting: grace and peace from God, and grace given in Christ Jesus. Everything the Corinthians possess, their standing, their speech, their knowledge, and their gifts, flows from grace, so that boasting in themselves is ruled out from the first sentence.

charisma.gloss: A gift of grace, a spiritual endowment freely given by God for service. The Corinthians lacked no gift, yet the letter shows that gifts without love and humility breed pride and division. The word shares its root with charis, marking every gift as an outflow of grace, not a badge of merit.

koinonia.gloss: Fellowship, sharing, communion, a genuine partnership of life. God has called the Corinthians into fellowship with His Son, a bond that both unites them to Christ and binds them to one another. This word quietly undercuts the party spirit Paul is about to confront: those who share in Christ cannot rightly splinter into rival camps.

pistos.gloss: Faithful, trustworthy, reliable, one who keeps his word. The paragraph ends on this word about God, who is faithful. The Corinthians' security to the end does not rest on their own steadfastness, which is fragile, but on the unbreakable faithfulness of the God who called them.

basic.context: Paul opens his letter to a church full of problems, and yet he begins not with rebuke but with grace. He reminds the Corinthians who they are: the church of God, set apart in Christ, called to be holy. He thanks God for the grace given to them and for the gifts and knowledge they enjoy. Then he assures them that God Himself will keep them to the end, blameless on the day of Christ, because God is faithful.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a standard letter opening, greeting and thanksgiving, but Paul loads it with themes he will develop through the whole letter: grace rather than merit, holiness, spiritual gifts, the coming day of Christ, and the faithfulness of God. Read it as the foundation. Everything he corrects later stands on the truth that they belong to God by grace.
basic.originalLanguages: They are hagiazo, sanctified, set apart in Christ. All they have is charis, grace. They lack no charisma, gift of grace. God has called them into koinonia, fellowship, with His Son. And the last word is pistos, faithful, spoken of God, who will keep them to the end.
basic.typology: The church is described as those called to be holy, the language once used of Israel set apart to the LORD; the true assembly of God is now gathered in Christ from Jew and Gentile alike. Being kept blameless for the day of the Lord takes up the prophets' longing for a purified people who can stand in God's presence.
basic.christ: Christ is named nine times in these nine verses. The Corinthians are sanctified in Christ Jesus, enriched in Him, awaiting His revelation, kept blameless for His day, and called into fellowship with Him. He is the center of their identity, their present enrichment, and their future hope.
basic.god: God is the source and the keeper. He gives grace, He confirms the testimony about Christ, He will sustain them to the end, and He is faithful. The security of believers rests finally on the character of God, not on the strength of their own grip.
basic.crossReferences: Philippians 1 gives thanks in almost identical terms and promises God will complete the good work He began. First Thessalonians speaks of God keeping His people blameless at the coming of Christ. Romans and Ephesians ground salvation in grace, not works, so that no one may boast.
basic.oneStory: God has always been gathering a people for Himself, set apart and kept by His own faithfulness. Here that people is the church in Corinth, sanctified in Christ and awaiting His return. The same faithful God who called them will bring them safely to the day of Christ, the goal of the whole story.

context.literary: This is the letter opening in the ancient form: sender, recipients, greeting, then thanksgiving. Paul expands each part theologically. The thanksgiving in verses 4 to 9 previews the letter's key words, grace, speech, knowledge, gifts, the day of Christ, so that the greeting doubles as a table of contents for what follows.
context.historical: Corinth was a wealthy, cosmopolitan Roman colony, proud of its rhetoric, status, and worldly wisdom. Paul had founded the church on his second missionary journey. Now, hearing of divisions and disorder, he writes from Ephesus. Sosthenes, likely the synagogue ruler of Acts 18, joins him as a witness to the church he once opposed.
context.canonical: The greeting sets Corinthian identity within the whole people of God, together with all those everywhere who call on the name of the Lord. It joins the New Testament pattern of opening thanksgivings and anchors the letter in the gospel of grace before addressing any error.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish the Corinthians' true identity and security in Christ by grace, and to give thanks for God's work among them, so that the hard corrections to come are received as words to a beloved and secure people, not as threats to their standing.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: These verses describe what God has done and declares to be true of the church; they are gospel indicative before any command. The implied call, to live as those sanctified and to await Christ, flows from the status God has already given, not the reverse.

debates.question: If God will keep them blameless to the end, why does Paul spend the rest of the letter correcting them so sharply?

views.label: Assurance grounds obedience
views.case: God's faithfulness to keep His people is the very reason to pursue holiness; grace is not a license to sin but the power and motive for repentance. The security is real, and it fuels the corrections rather than making them pointless.

views.label: Perseverance is worked out through the corrections
views.case: God keeps His people precisely by means of apostolic warning and discipline; the letter is one of the instruments through which the faithful God sustains them blameless to the day of Christ.

debates.bearing: Both readings hold that final security rests on God's faithfulness, not human merit, and that this assurance drives, rather than dissolves, the call to holiness.

study.originalLanguages: The greeting is saturated with grace. The Corinthians are hagiazo, sanctified, set apart in Christ Jesus, a status God gives before they act. Twice charis, grace, sounds out: grace and peace from God, and grace given in Christ. They lack no charisma, gift of grace, the word rooted in charis to mark every endowment as gift, not achievement. God has called them into koinonia, fellowship, real partnership with His Son and so with one another. And the paragraph rests at last on pistos, faithful, the character of the God who will keep them to the end.

typology.fulfillment: The language of a people set apart to the LORD, once spoken over Israel, is now fulfilled in the church of God gathered in Christ from every place, Jew and Gentile together.

typology.type: Kept blameless for the day of the Lord
typology.fulfillment: The prophets' hope of a purified people able to stand before God is realized in those whom the faithful God sustains blameless for the day of Christ.

study.christ: Jesus Christ dominates the opening, named repeatedly as the sphere and source of everything. The Corinthians are sanctified in Christ Jesus, enriched in Him, confirmed by the testimony about Him, awaiting the revelation of Him, kept blameless for His day, and called into fellowship with Him. Paul's very apostleship is of Christ Jesus by the will of God. From the first line, Christ is not one theme among many but the ground of the church's identity, its present life, and its future hope.
study.god: God is the initiator and the sustainer. It is God's will that made Paul an apostle, God's church that gathers in Corinth, God's grace that enriches them, God who confirmed the testimony about Christ, and God who will sustain them to the end. The final word, faithful, reveals His character: the One who calls also keeps. Assurance is therefore not presumption but confidence in the trustworthiness of God.

crossReferences.note: A parallel thanksgiving and the promise that God will complete the good work He began, matching the confidence of verses 8 and 9.
God who calls is faithful and will keep His people blameless at the coming of Christ, echoing verse 9.
Salvation is by grace, not works, so that no one may boast, grounding the grace-centered greeting.
God is able to keep His people from stumbling and present them blameless, the same keeping power Paul assures here.

study.oneStory: From the beginning God has been calling out a people for Himself, setting them apart and keeping them by His own faithfulness. In Corinth that people takes the form of a flawed but genuine church, sanctified in Christ and enriched by grace. The faithful God who called them into fellowship with His Son will not abandon them but will bring them safe to the day of Christ, the destination toward which the whole biblical story moves.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe the Corinthians in the greeting, before addressing any of their problems?

options.text: As sanctified in Christ Jesus and called to be holy, the church of God.
options.note: He grounds them in their God-given identity by grace before any correction.

options.text: As a gifted church that simply needed some fine-tuning.
options.note: They were richly gifted, but Paul's first word is their holy standing in Christ, not their gifting.

options.text: As people who had earned God's favor by their spiritual maturity.
options.note: Their standing rests on grace and God's call, not earned merit; the letter exposes their immaturity.

options.text: As unbelievers with no real relationship to God.
options.note: Paul calls them the church of God, sanctified in Christ and given every grace.

quiz.q: On what basis does Paul say the Corinthians will be kept blameless to the end?

options.text: On the faithfulness of God, who called them into fellowship with His Son.
options.note: Verse 9 grounds their security in God's faithfulness, not their own strength.

options.text: On their careful use of their spiritual gifts.
options.note: They were richly gifted, but their keeping rests on God, not on their gifts.

options.text: On their steady progress in holiness over time.
options.note: The letter shows their progress was faltering; God's faithfulness is the true ground.

options.text: On their own determination to hold on to the end.
options.note: Their security rests on the faithful God who sustains them, not on their grip.

quiz.q: Why does Paul give thanks for the Corinthians' speech, knowledge, and gifts?

options.text: Because they are all evidence of the grace God has given them in Christ.
options.note: Verses 4 to 7 trace every enrichment back to grace given in Christ Jesus.

options.text: Because these achievements proved how spiritually advanced they were.
options.note: The gifts are real, but Paul credits grace, not their achievement, and later warns against pride.

options.text: Because their gifts made them superior to other churches.
options.note: The gifts are gifts of grace for service, not a badge of superiority.

options.text: Because they had generated these gifts by their own effort.
options.note: Every gift is charisma, an outflow of grace freely given by God.

quiz.q: What does the word fellowship (koinonia) in verse 9 imply for a divided church?

options.text: That those who share in Christ are bound together and should not splinter into rival camps.
options.note: Fellowship with the Son binds believers to one another, undercutting the coming party spirit.

options.text: That believers simply attend the same gatherings from time to time.
options.note: Koinonia is deeper than attendance; it is a genuine partnership of life in Christ.

options.text: That each believer relates to Christ privately with no bond to others.
options.note: Fellowship with the Son necessarily joins believers to one another as well.

options.text: That fellowship depends on all agreeing about their favorite teacher.
options.note: The bond is in Christ Himself, not in loyalty to human leaders.

questions: Paul reminds the Corinthians who they are in Christ before addressing what is wrong. How might remembering your identity in Christ change the way you face your own faults?
Everything the Corinthians had was grace, freely given. Where are you tempted to treat your abilities or knowledge as achievements to boast in rather than gifts to steward?
Your security rests on the faithfulness of God, not the strength of your grip on Him. How does that truth steady you when your own faith feels weak?
God has called you into fellowship with His Son and so with His people. What would it look like to take that bond with other believers as seriously as Paul does here?
Paul lived with the day of Christ in view. How would eagerly awaiting Christ's return reshape your priorities this week?

translationNotes.note: Sosthenes is likely the synagogue ruler of Acts 18:17, who was beaten in Corinth; if so, his appearance here as Paul's brother is a quiet testimony to the gospel's power to transform a former opponent.
Called to be holy renders the same root as sanctified earlier in the verse; the BSB captures both the settled status, set apart in Christ, and the ongoing calling to live it out.
The day of our Lord Jesus Christ echoes the Old Testament day of the LORD, now centered on Christ's return, when His people will be found blameless.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 1:1-9. Any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas per the no-dash style rule; wording is otherwise unchanged.
